questions.text:
QUESTION TWO  
Write a note on any two of the following: The rules and principles that dictate how an intestate’s estate should be distributed. Section 117 of the Succession Act 1965, which imposes a legal duty upon parents to make ‘proper provisions’ for their children during the parents’ lifetime or from their estate after death. The circumstances in which indirect contributions to the purchase of a house by a non-owning spouse may lead to them acquiring an equity in the property.

questions.description: Nell and bob are two trainee solicitors living and working in cork. One evening, Nell watches a documentary on metal det…
questions.text: Nell and bob are two trainee solicitors living and working in cork. One evening, Nell watches a documentary on metal detecting and the value at auction of some of the artefacts that have been found by amateur detectorists. Thinking that this would be a handy way to make a bit more money, Nell buys two second hand metal detectors online. After a bit of convincing, Bob agrees to join Nell in searching for valuable buried objects on a nearby farm. After an hour of finding nothing more than rusty nails, Bob’s metal detector beeps loudly. Bob starts digging behind a shed – after a bit of huffing and puffing, Bob sees something glinting and discovers that he has found a 600-year-old golden goblet. Bob carefully places it in a small bag that he has brought. Pleased with their find, Nell and Bob decide to head home for a closer look. As they do, they bump into the farmer, who asks them what they are doing. They show him the goblet. The farmer thinks the goblet is just a children’s toy and dismisses it – he also says in passing that he is only renting the land from his father-in-law, who actually owns the land on which the farm sits. Advise Nell and Bob as to whether they can keep their find.

questions.description: Mary and her sister Susan decide that they are ging to acquire a small bungalow that they will live in together. They ac…
questions.text: Mary and her sister Susan decide that they are ging to acquire a small bungalow that they will live in together. They acquire the property as joint tenants. After having lived together relatively peacefully for 5 years, tensions start to grow between the sister, with Susan’s messiness aggravating Mary and making her increasingly angry. One morning, after finding three days’ worth of Susan’s breakfast bowls sitting beside the sink, Mary snaps and starts yelling at Susan. When susan tells her to stop being such an idiot, Mary grabs a kitchen knife and plunges it into Susan’s torso, killing her instantly. What will happen to the property of Susan that she held with Mary as a joint tenant while she was alive? Do you think this is fair and are there any alternatives/reforms that you would propose?

questions.description: Fred bought his semi-detached house in 2007. At the time, it was a little run down, and Fred looked forward to doing it …
questions.text: Fred bought his semi-detached house in 2007. At the time, it was a little run down, and Fred looked forward to doing it up over time. In 2020, he decided that he had had enough of the rickety conservatory and decided that he was going to have a light and airy extension built. In order to fund the not insignificant cost of a new extension, Fred re-mortgaged his property with Ireland Bank PLC. Building work started in January 2021, with the total removal of the back wall. However, since December 2020, Fred has been struggling financially – his hours have been reduced at work and he is struggling to meet his bills. Since April 2021, therefore, he has been struggling to pay the builders (who downed tools in July 221 after not being paid for several months). Fred tried to make some mortgage payments for a few months, but has not made any mortgage payments since June 2021. Ireland Bank PLC has sent several letters to Fred over the last 15 months about his non payment of the mortgage. These letters each flagged that if Fred did not start repaying in the following month, they would repossess his home. Fred has become very stressed by the situation and after reading the first warning letter from Ireland Bank PLC has been ignoring these letters, shoving them behind the toaster without even opening them. Fred comes to you for advice as to what his options are. He indicates to you that if there is no alternative but for Ireland Bank PLC to sell his home, he would at least like to have the extension finished before it is sold. Please advise Fred, with reference to relevant legal authority as appropriate.

questions.description: Piers is a landlord of a small commercial unit, which he rents to Beth who runs a pottery shop in the unit. While she st…
questions.text: Piers is a landlord of a small commercial unit, which he rents to Beth who runs a pottery shop in the unit. While she started out as the model tenant, over the last 6 months, Beth has breached a number of covenants in the lease, including breaching the covenant to pay the rent on a monthly basis. Piers comes to you for advice as to what he can do. Please set out in detail the forfeiture procedure in Ireland. Can, and if so how, might the procedure be used to terminate Beth’s lease by forfeiture?

questions.description: During the first Covid-19 lockdown in May 2020, Blanche went to her holiday home in the West of Ireland to shut herself …
questions.text: During the first Covid-19 lockdown in May 2020, Blanche went to her holiday home in the West of Ireland to shut herself away from everyone. One afternoon, she took her dog for a walk in the local area and stumbled on something which appeared to be sticking out from the ground. She retrieved a large bronze object from the ground which was covered in soil. Blanche was always of the belief that “finders' keepers, losers' weepers”. However, since she is an honest person, she decided to tell the owner of a nearby B & B, Kenneth, who informed her that the land doesn’t actually belong to him, but to his sister Julia, who took out a 99-year lease on the land a couple of years ago. Kenneth suggests that perhaps his sister is probably entitled to it. Advise Blanche.

questions.description: Jack and Jill were joint tenants of a house in the Dublin suburbs. Last year, they were travelling on an airplane which …
questions.text: Jack and Jill were joint tenants of a house in the Dublin suburbs. Last year, they were travelling on an airplane which crashed. Both were killed at the same instant when the plane on which they were both travelling struck a mountain. By her will, Jill left all her property to her friend, Julie. By his will, Jack left all his property to his two siblings Anne and Barry. Advise the respective executors as to who is entitled to the house. AND Bartholomew and Margaret established and operated an estate agent together and, in 2015, decided to purchase a property in Wicklow to accommodate their practice. Each put €750,000 towards the purchase price of €1,500,000 and the property was conveyed “to Bartholomew and Margaret”. Relations have recently become strained between the two of them and Bartholomew wishes to set up his own business in Dublin. He wants to force a sale of the property or to make Margaret pay him a rent if Margaret continues to occupy the premises. Bartholomew has recently had a heart bypass operation and is also concerned as to whether his family would have a claim to the property if he happened to die before matters were resolved. Advise the parties.

questions.description: In March 2020, Phil, an elderly lady, discovered that she had an unusual lump in her leg. Upon further investigation at …
questions.text: In March 2020, Phil, an elderly lady, discovered that she had an unusual lump in her leg. Upon further investigation at the hospital, it was decided that the lump was cancerous, should be removed as soon as possible and further treatment would be required. Before Phil went into hospital for treatment, she decided that she had better draw up a will making provision for her husband, Brian. She didn’t feel there was any need to include her son, Dylan, as she believed that when Brian died, Dylan would ultimately inherit everything. Phil asked her solicitor, Liz, if she could draw up the will according to her instructions and drop it by her house for signature the week before the operation was due to take place. However, a few days later, Phil won €50,000 on a scratch card which she collected. She decided that it would be important to include this in her will also. When Liz called to the house with the will, Phil signed the will in the presence of Liz and Dylan. Brian was walking into the room next door when Phil was initialing the will. “I think you are supposed to watch me doing this love!” Phil called out to him. “I can see you signing it darling” he replied as he had his back turned. After Liz left the house, Phil suddenly remembered the money that she had won on the scratch card and added it to the bottom of the will, indicating that it was to go to Dylan. She thought that she should get her signature witnessed, so asked both Dylan and his friend (who happened to be there at the time) to sign the will. Later that evening, Brian came in and sat by the fire. It was nearly gone out so he threw some old newspapers into the fire to try and keep it going. The will which was stuck inside the bundle of newspapers was thrown into the fire and destroyed. That night, Phil died in her sleep. Dylan claims that he is entitled to the €50,000 that his mother intended to leave to him, but Brian disagrees. Advise Brian and Dylan as to the terms and validity of the will.

questions.description: In 2018, Julie mortgaged her house to an Irish Bank so that she could convert the attic in her home into an additional g…
questions.text: In 2018, Julie mortgaged her house to an Irish Bank so that she could convert the attic in her home into an additional guestroom. Since the onset of the covid-19 pandemic in March 2020 however, Julie has been struggling financially and has been unable to pay the builders to complete the conversion. Moreover, Julie has not made any mortgage repayments for the last 14 months. The Irish Bank has sent Julie a number of warning letters explaining that if she does not begin to make repayments within the next month, the bank will have no choice but to take possession of her home with a view to putting it on the market. After reading the first of these letters, Julie placed the remaining bank letters unopened in her drawer. Julie comes to you seeking advice on her position in light of the above facts. She outlines her preference that if the house has to be sold, that the Irish Bank wait until she completes the attic conversion. Advise Julie.

questions.description: ‘[Adverse Possession's] controversy largely derives from a particular feature of its operation, whereby, to put it somew…
questions.text:
‘[Adverse Possession's] controversy largely derives from a particular feature of its operation, whereby, to put it somewhat crudely, a trespasser on someone’s land may, if the trespass continues without disturbance for the statutory limitation period, become the owner of the land possessed. What appears extraordinary is that the legal system seems to be sanctioning and rewarding the committal of a civil wrong, without any question of compensation being paid to the dispossessed owner. This feature [has] assumed greater significance in recent times because of the emphasis on constitutional protection of property rights and human rights generally.’ JCW Wylie, ‘Adverse Possession: Still an Ailing Concept?’ (2017) 58 Irish Jurist (n.s.) 1.
Discuss. Your answer should consider whether adverse possession can be justified and, furthermore, if you would abolish, reform or leave untouched the Irish law of Adverse Possession.

questions.text: According to the Property Registration Authority, By international comparison, Ireland has a very extensive and well-developed system of land registration. Since the foundation of the Land Registry in 1892, there has been a gradual, ongoing and continuous program of movement away from the older and limited system of recording Deeds (in the registry of Deeds) to the more modern, flexible and comprehensive ‘title registration’ system provided through the Land Registry. Considering the above statement, describe and discuss the system for recording property transactions in Ireland.

questions.description: Mary is 65 years old and for the past 30 years she has been employed as a housekeeper for Bridie at Bridie’s house. When…
questions.text:
Mary is 65 years old and for the past 30 years she has been employed as a housekeeper for Bridie at Bridie’s house. When she first started working for Bridie, Mary received £35 a week, and this was never increased. Over the years, Bridie became increasingly dependent on Mary for things that went above and beyond the role of housekeeper. In fact, Mary had become Bridie's carer rather down her housekeeper. Despite the fact that there was no written agreement to this effect, Mary claims that Bridie had led her to believe that upon Bridie's death, Mary would be entitled to the house, or at the very least a right to remain in it. Mary stated that this was a mutual understanding between the parties. Bridie has now died and has left all of her property to the cats and dog's home.
Mary seeks your advice on whether she has a claim to any interest in the house.

questions.text: The Residential Tenancies Act 2004 (as amended) when first enacted, contained a number of significant reforms for the private sector in Ireland, in particular, in the area of security of tenure, and tenancy termination procedures. Consider whether the Residential Tenancies Act 2004 has succeeded in its aim of providing a clear legislative framework for tenancy obligations for landlords and tenants in Ireland.

Gerry has recently had a heart bypass operation and is concerned about whether his family would have a claim to the property if he happened to die before matters are resolved. Advise Gerry.

questions.description: Harry and Sally got married in 2012. They moved into their new home in Athlone, which was located on 100-acre farm in th…
questions.text: Harry and Sally got married in 2012. They moved into their new home in Athlone, which was located on 100-acre farm in the countryside. Both the family home in the farm were in Harry's name. In 2015, Sally discovered that Harry was having an extra-marital affair with her best friend and so she moved back in with her parents in Cork. Harry distracted himself with the farm work and decided that an expansion of the business would be a good idea. He applied to take out a mortgage with the bank. When the bank asks if he is married, Harry replied, “My wife is dead to me”. The bank took this literally and suggested that Harry would have to sign the statutory declaration to say that his wife was deceased in order to proceed with his application. Harry secured the mortgage. However, over time, the reality of the marriage breakdown hit Harry and he lost all interest in everything, including the farm. This resulted in him defaulting on his mortgage repayments from 2019. Sally recently became aware through Harry’s sister that the bank now intends to sell the farm including the family home. Sally is furious and claims she never consented to this mortgage on the property. Advise Sally.

questions.description: When Louis discovered he had a terminal illness in January 2019, he decided to draft a will. At the time, he was undergo…
questions.text: When Louis discovered he had a terminal illness in January 2019, he decided to draft a will. At the time, he was undergoing an aggressive form of treatment and so he didn't have the energy to go to his solicitor. He asked his husband, Harvey, to download a free will template from a website he had heard about. Louis invited their close neighbors, Tom and Jerry, over for dinner that evening with him and Harvey. Over a glass of wine after dinner, Louis began filling out a number of versions of his will. Once he was happy with the contents of the will of (€150,000 to his husband, Harvey, €20,000 to his niece, Donna and the remainder to the local animal rescue service), he asked Tom and Jerry if they could sign their names to the document. When Tom and Jerry left, Louis began tidying up. When disposing of the many versions of the will he had drafted and ripped up, he accidentally threw the proper version in the bin. The next morning, when Harvey realized Louis mistake, he cello-taped the will back together and put it in the drawer. In June 2020 comment Louis sadly passed away. Harvey recently brought the will to a local solicitors office where questions concerning the content and validity of the will have been raised. Advise Harvey.

questions.text: Jackie died suddenly from a short illness in January 2019. At the time of her death, she was living on the family farm (which she had inherited some 20 years previous) with her wife Julia. In her will, Jackie has left all of her worldly belongings to her son, Matthew. Jackie also has a daughter, Mary, from a previous relationship to whom she left nothing under the will. Upon discovering her mother’s death, Mary claims she was left out of the will as she did not get along with her mother. However, she does acknowledge that her mother gave her €15,000 10 years ago to start up a new business venture which never took off. In fact, Mary was challenged by alcohol and drug addiction at the time and mishandled the money as a result. Mary has been in recovery for the past five years and is currently unemployed and living in local authority housing. Matthew, on the other hand, is married with three children and is a wealthy dentist. Advise Matthew and Mary.

questions.description: In 2004, Ann decided to start growing some organic vegetables in a field behind her house. After she had them all plante…
questions.text:
In 2004, Ann decided to start growing some organic vegetables in a field behind her house. After she had them all planted, she had a small shed erected which she used as a storage space for her gardening tools. A couple of months later, Ann planted a large hedge along the boundary to act as a windbreak. Barry, the registered owner of the field, lived in Spain.
While holidaying in Ireland in 2016, Barry drove past the field only to see all the work that Ann had done on it. Ann happened to be out pulling up some weeds at the time. Barry told Ann that he had no present use for the field so she could continue her great work but he did intend to seek planning permission to build a house on it in the future. In the meantime, Barry told Ann that he would be happy to grant her a short lease at a nominal fee. Ann asked him to write to her solicitor about this but despite the fact that negotiations followed, no lease was ever granted. Ann died in 2018, leaving all her property to her daughter, Sheila, who resided with her. Barry died in 2014, leaving all his property to his son Dermot. Dermot is in poor health and lives in a nursing home. Sheila seeks your advice on whether or not she can be registered as owner of the field.

questions.description: Following a night out with some friends in the local village, David decided to take a short cut home through a field whi…
questions.text: Following a night out with some friends in the local village, David decided to take a short cut home through a field which was on the way to his house. While trespassing on this property, he tripped over something which was half buried in the soil. Upon further investigation, he realised that he had discovered what appeared to be a very valuable silver chalice. Margaret, who had taken out a ninety-nine-year lease of the field the previous year, claimed that she should be entitled to the find. As word got around the local village, Margaret’s landlord, Tim, heard about the find and claimed that since he owned the field, he should be entitled to the chalice. However, David now claims that since he was the one to discover the chalice, he should be entitled to keep it. Advise David, citing relevant legal authority in your answer.

questions.description: Tom owns a house in the city centre of Dublin which he purchased in 1991. Just beyond the boundary of his garden is a sm…
questions.text: Tom owns a house in the city centre of Dublin which he purchased in 1991. Just beyond the boundary of his garden is a small patch of waste land, which is owned by Dublin City Council. Tom has been parking his car on this waste land since he purchased his house. From about 1995, he began cultivating this place of waste land as a garden and around this time he constructed some temporary sheds on the land. In 2006, concerned about anti-social behaviour of some youths who had taken to loitering on the strip of land, he constructed a wall around it. In 2015, the Council wrote to Tom offering to lease the land to him at a low rent on condition that it is only used as a garden. Tom replied by letter stating simply that he would consider the proposal. There had been no further correspondence until recently, with the Council threatening to evict Tom from the strip of land as he is trespassing, and the land is needed as part of a development project by the Council to widen a nearby road. Advise Tom.

questions.text: Mary holds the lease on a small unit in Dublin City Centre which she runs as a newsagents. The lease only contains clauses indicating the term ( of four years) of the lease, together with a covenant for the payment of rent. Due to the recession, Mary’s business has not been performing as well as expected and she has missed a number of rent payments. On the 1st March she was served with a notice by the landlord requiring her to clear the arrears of rent within 24 hours, and this notice stated that a failure to do so would result in her forfeiting the lease. Two days after the notice was served the landlord and his agents broke the locks of the unit and entered the premises overnight. They clumsily removed all of the stock, together with the fixtures and fittings of the newsagent, and dumped it in storage for her to collect. She is now excluded from her business, and comes to you seeking urgent advice as to any remedy she might have against the landlord. Advise Mary on any legal remadies that may be available to her.

The wall will cut off access to the road from the field and it will considerably reduce the amount of light received through the conservatory and destroy Tom’s view of the woods. Advise Tom.

Joe, thinking this would make a lovely gift for his father who was a dedicated antique collector, dropped the watch into his pocket. When he looked up, Joe saw that Alex (who sold Tom the land ten years ago) and Tom were both standing at the entrance to the field. questions.text: In March 2013, Mel, who was a fit and healthy 28-year-old, noticed that she had an unusual mark on her skin. Upon further investigation at the hospital, it was determined that Mel had skin melanoma (cancer), which needed to be removed as soon as possible and further treatment would be required. Before Mel went into hospital for treatment, her boyfriend, Mark, who was a solicitor, suggested that it may be a good idea to draw up a will since she had three-year old twins. Being a positive thinker and not really dwelling on it too much, Mel decided to draft a will for the sake of keeping Mark happy. She asked Mark to act as her solicitor by drawing up a preliminary will leaving all of her worldly items to her twin sons. Mark did so and dropped it by her house for signature the week before the operation was due to take place. However, a few days later, Mel entered a competition on the Weekend Show and won a car with €20,000 in the glovebox. She decided that it would be important to include this in her will also. When Mark called to the house the day before her operation to collect the signed will, Mel signed the original will document and added in handwriting, the car and money. She did this in the presence of Mark and her twin boys. Her neighbour Sheila was also walking into the room when Mel was initialling the will. “Sheila, you need to see this too! Mark called out to her. “I can see you signing it Mel” she replied as she had her back turned pouring tea. Later that evening, Sheila and Mel were alone sitting by the fire. The fire was nearly gone out so Sheila threw some old newspapers into the fire to try and keep it going. It was only after she threw in the newspaper that she noticed that the documents of the will were stuck in between the papers. She quickly removed the will from the fire, with the document being only partially burned. Mel’s condition rapidly deteriorated after she stopped responding to treatment and she died a few weeks later. Advise as the terms and validity of the will.

questions.text: In 1988, Fran inherited a small cottage from her uncle. She visited the property on a couple of occasions but decided that she did not wish to live there as it was much too quiet and isolated in her job and friends were located in the city. Nonetheless, she decided to keep the house as somewhere to go for a break at weekends, a holiday home in the summer and ultimately a retirement home. She asked the neighbour next door – Charles – to ‘keep an eye’ on the cottage for her and gave him a spare key in the event an emergency arose. However, work became very busy for Fran that year, the result of which was that she never had the chance to stay in the cottage. A couple of years later, Charles began using the shed at the rear of the cottage to store his bicycle and his garden tools. In 1994, he decided to carry out some repairs on the shed as well as the grounds which had become quite dilapidated and in a state of disrepair. This work, which included a new gate and padlock (for which he had the only key) cost him €500. Since Fran had not been in contact with Charles, he never told her about this. A year later, Charles started to use the house to accommodate friends who came to visit. He also started to rent it out during the summer months and kept the money himself. In 2013, Charles sold his land to Jack and handed over the keys to Fran’s house. He informed Jack that Fran had not been around for years. Jack continued to use Fran’s property as if it was his own. In early 2018, Fran returned to her cottage and demanded her keys back. Advise Jack.

questions.text: In 2014, four close college friends, David, Paul, Matthew and Alex, bought a duplex apartment together. Title to the apartment was not registered in the Land Registry. They each paid an equal share of the purchase price. Shortly afterwards, David lost his job and decided to emigrate and sold his interest to Paul. In 2015, Paul unexpectedly became a father and decided, without legal advice, that it was a good time to make a will whereby he would leave all of his share in the property to his son. In 2016, Matthew moved out and rented a room in a house a few doors away because he was finding it very difficult to study for his final year exams in the apartment due to elevated noise levels. This caused an argument between Alex and Paul which resulted in Paul removing all of Alex’s possessions, including his plasma screen television and his X Box, from the living room and locking the living room door. On hearing of this, Alex’s girlfriend suggested that he live with her in her house and so Alex moved out. Matthew and Alex believe that Paul should pay them rent. Alternatively, Matthew suggests that the apartment should be sold. Alex is uncertain as to whether the apartment should be sold because its market value still is not quite what it was when they purchased it. Paul says that he is not willing to pay them rent and that they are welcome to stay in the apartment. He objects to selling the apartment because he will not be able to find another home in the vicinity that is suitable for him given its close proximity to his place of work. He is also concerned as to whether his son would be adequately provided for if he were to die unexpectedly. Advise the parties.

questions.text: When Olive discovered that she had a terminal illness in January 2018, she decided to draft a will. At the time, she was undergoing an aggressive form of treatment and so she didn’t have the energy to go to her solicitor. She asked he civil partner of four years, Nicola, to download a free will template from a website she had heard about. Olive invited her close neighbours, Tom and Jerry, over for dinner that evening with her and Nicola. Over a glass of wine after dinner, Olive began filling in a number of versions of her will. Once she was happy with the contents (€150,000 to her partner Nicola, €20,000 to her niece, Julie and the remainder to the local animal rescue service), she asked Tom and Jerry if they could sign their names to the document. When Tom and Jerry left, Olive began tidying up. When disposing of the many versions of the will she had drafted, she accidentally threw the proper version in the bin. The next morning, when Nicola realised Olive’s mistakes, she cello-taped the will back together and put in the drawer. In June 2018, Olive sadly passed away. Nicola recently brought the will to a local solicitor’s office where she has been met with questions concerning the content and validity of the will. Advise Nicola.

questions.text: Harry and Sally got married in 2012. They moved into their new home in Athlone which was located on a 100-acre farm in the countryside. Both the family home and the farm were in Harry’s name. In 2015, Sally discovered that Harry was having an extra-marital affair with her best friend and so she moved back in with her parents in Cork. Harry distracted himself with the farm work and decided that an expansion of the business would be a good idea. He applied to take out a mortgage with the bank. When the bank asked if he was married, Harry replied ‘My wife is dead to me.’ The bank took this literally and suggested that Harry would have to sign the statutory declaration to say that his wife was deceased in order to proceed with his application. Harry secured the mortgage. However, over time, the reality of the marriage breakdown hit Harry and he lost all interest in everything including the farm. This resulted in him defaulting on his mortgage repayments from 2016. Sally recently became aware through Harry’s sister that the Bank now intends to sell the farm including the family home. Sally is furious and claims she never consented to this mortgage on the property. Advise Sally.

In 2007, Jones installed a gate at the entrance to the property to which he has the only key. The Council is now about to sell the land to the Department of Education and it is planned to build a school on the site. Jones has just initiated proceedings claiming adverse possession of the land. Advise Kildare County Council.

In 2017, Neil learned that Claire had sold her field to Anlex Limited which plans to build a large warehouse and depot on the field. Neil is also concerned that the large warehouse and depot to be built by Anlex Limited may impede the flow of light to his greenhouse. Advise Neil.

questions.text: Jim dies on the 1 st April 2017, at the age of 75 years leaving a wife, Samantha, and their two children, Molly, aged 16, a schoolgirl, and Mandy, aged 26, an electrical engineer; as well as Samantha's child from a previous relationship, Leanne, aged 32, whom Jim adopted when she was a baby. Jim paid for Leanne's University education, but she has had problems with alcohol since she was young and dropped out of college after a couple of years. Prior to her wedding she had planned on purchasing a house with her husband. Her father had found out about this and said to her "Sure why would you do that? This house will be yours and Molly's after your mother's time." (Jim was referring to the family home which was solely owned by him). Mandy never went through with the purchase of the house, but Jim never spoke of the matter again. In Jim's will, Samantha is left a token sum of €1 ,000 and nothing else. He leaves all of the residue of the estate, including his house, to Molly. Advise the parties on the legal issues that are likely to arise in relation to succession to Jim's estate.

questions.description: In 2002, Jack, a farmer, sold one of his fields to Champion Developments Ltd. The field was adjacent to a dual carriagew…
questions.text:
In 2002, Jack, a farmer, sold one of his fields to Champion Developments Ltd. The field was adjacent to a dual carriageway which the local authorities had indicated would be upgraded to motorway status in the near future. Champion Developments Ltd bought the property from Jack with the intention of building a large motorway service station on the site. Once the sale of the land was complete, Champion Developments Ltd wrote a letter to Jack asking him if he could remove his cattle from the field. They also requested that he place one of their advertising signs on the gate nearest the road. However, Jack, being a fairly laid back type of person, left these tasks on the long finger. In fact, due to various delays in the motorway development, Jack continued to graze his cattle on the land for a number of years. In 2008, Jack left farming, sold his sheep and retired to Turkey. Before he left, he made an agreement to lease the field to his neighbour, Frank, so that he could grow some mushrooms. Frank erected a number of structures in the field to serve as growing rooms.
In 2010, the Chief Executive of Champion Developments Ltd was driving by the field and noticed that there was some kind of unusual looking structure in it. He parked the car and looked in over the gate at the structure in the field. He decided to notify his solicitor who wrote to Frank requesting that he leave this private property immediately. Frank replied and said he had intended on leaving anyway as he was getting tired of the mushroom farming and in any case, more traffic on a motorway would ruin his crops. At the beginning of this year, Champion Developments Ltd has finally started on the motorway development but Frank has decided he wants to remain on the land to try and grow more organic vegetables. Advise Champion Developments Ltd.

questions.description: Matthew died last month from a severe heart attack, leaving a net estate worth €500,000. Matthew made a will in 2005. Th…
questions.text: Matthew died last month from a severe heart attack, leaving a net estate worth €500,000. Matthew made a will in 2005. The only bequest in the will was one of "€50,000 to my best friend, Paul Simpson". However, in the will, the figure "€50,000" has been crossed out and the figure "€100,000" written above it in black pen (the rest of the will had been typewritten). Matthew's solicitor now admits that there was a mistake in the drafting of the will. The week after Matthew had first given instructions as to the terms of the will, the solicitor's secretary had received a phone call from Matthew saying that he felt he should leave more money to his dear friend Paul and asking the solicitor to change the amount of the bequest to Paul Simpson to €100,000. Unfortunately, the secretary, who was leaving to go on holidays that day, completely forgot to pass the message on to the solicitor. The solicitor is not sure how the figure of €100,000 came to appear on the will but believes that it is in the testator's handwriting. One of the witnesses to the will, Jenny, was cohabiting with Paul when the will was executed and the couple were married two months later. Matthew was a bachelor and had no children. His parents both died twenty years ago. However, he was survived by one of his sisters, Julie, with whom he had lost touch. His other twin sisters, Jackie and Ellie, died ten years ago. At the time of Matthew's death, Jackie was survived by her children, Jack and Jill, and Ellie was survived by her two grandchildren, Tom and Jerry. Advise as to the proper distribution of Matthew's estate.

questions.text: One Friday evening, while out exploring with his dog on his father's land, 15 year old Julien discovered a hidden entrance to an old disused mine which was accessible from both his father's land and that of his neighbour, Sydney. Julien and his dog walked over a worn and muddy cardboard sign on the ground which read "Keep out - Danger". However, Julien and his dog carried on regardless and ventured further into the mine. After a few minutes, Julien stumbled upon a shiny object which appeared to be gold as well as some other pieces of old fashioned jewellery which were half buried in the ground. He wiped off the soil and saw that on the back of it there was an engraving "All my love to my dearest A, always and forever". Julien dropped the object and the other artefacts into the pocket of his jacket. Just then, Jake, who had sold Julien's father the land 12 years previously, appeared in the mine with Julien's father and claimed that he should have the find as it belonged to him. Advise the parties as to who is entitled to the discovered objects.

questions.text: In 2007, four close college friends, David, Paul, Matthew and Alex, bought a duplex apartment together. Title to the apartment was not registered in the Land Registry. They each paid an equal share of the purchase price. Shortly afterwards, David lost his job and decided to emigrate and sold his interest to Paul. In 2006, Paul unexpectedly became and decided, without legal advice, that it was a good time to a make a will whereby he would leave all of his share in the property to his son. In 2009, Matthew moved out and rented a room in a house a few doors away because he was it very difficult to study for his final exams in the apartment due to elevated noise levels. This caused an argument between Alex and Paul which resulted in Paul removing all of Alex’s possessions, including his plasma screen television and his X Box, from the living room and locking the living room door. On hearing of this, Alex's girlfriend suggested that he live with her in her house and so Alex then moved out. Matthew and Alex believe that should pay them cent. Alternatively, Matthew suggests that the apartment should be sold. Alex is uncertain as to whether the apartment should be sold because its market value has fallen a great deal over the past couple of years due to the recession. Paul says that he is not willing to pay them rent and that they are welcome to stay in the apartment. He objects to selling the apartment because he will not be able to find another home in the vicinity that is suitable for him given its close proximity to his place of work. He is also concerned as to whether his son would be adequately provided for if he were to die unexpectedly. Advise the parties.

questions.text: In 1980, Jenny inherited a small cottage from her uncle. She visited the property on a couple of occasions but decided that she did not wish to live there as it was much too quiet and isolated and her job friends were located in the city. Nonetheless, she decided to keep the house as somewhere to go for a break some weekends, holiday home in the summer and ultimately a retirement home. She asked the neighbour next door – Charles - ‘to keep an eye’ on the cottage for her and gave him a spare key in the event that an emergency arose. However, work became very busy for Jenny that year, the result of which was that she never had the chance to stay in the cottage. A couple of years later, Charles began using the shed at the rear of the cottage to store his bicycle and his garden tools. In 1985 he decided to out some carry out some repairs on the shed as well as the grounds which had become quite dilapidated and in a state of disrepair. This work, which included a new gate and padlock (for which he had the only key) cost him €500. Since Jenny had not been in contact with Charles, he never told her about this. A year later, Charles stacked to use the house to accommodate friends who came to visit. He also staffed to rent it out during the summer months and kept the money for himself. In 2005, Charles sold his land to Jack end handed over the keys to Jenny's house. He informed Jack that Jenny had not been around for years. Jack continued to use Jenny’s property as if it was his own. In early 2010, Jenny returned to her cottage and demanded her keys back. Advise Jack.

questions.text: In March 2011, Julia, who was an elderly lady, discovered that she had a lump in her leg. Upon further investigation at the hospital, it was decided that the lump was cancerous, should be removed as soon as possible and further treatment would be required. Before Julia went into hospital for treatment, she decided that she had better draw up a will making provision for her husband, Jeff. She didn’t feel there was any need to include her son, Dylan, as she believed that when Jeff died, Dylan would ultimately inherit everything. Julia asked her solicitor, Mary, if she could draw up the will according to her instructions and drop it by her house for signature the week before the operation was due to take place. However, a few days later Julia won €50,000 on a scratch card which she collected. She decided that it would be important to include this in her will also. When Mary called to the house with the will, Julia signed the will in the presence of Mary and Dylan. Jeff was walking into the room next door when Julia was initialling the will. “I think you are supposed to watch me doing this love!” Julia called out to him. “I can see you signing it darling” he replied as he had his back turned. After Mary left the house, Julia suddenly remembered the money that she had won on the scratch card and added it to the bottom of the will, indicating that it was to go to Dylan. She thought that she should get her signature witnessed so asked both Dylan and his friend (who happened to be there at the time) to sign the will. Later that evening, Jeff came in and sat by the fire and keep it going. The will which was struck amidst the newspapers that were thrown into the fire was destroyed as a result. That night, Julia died in her sleep. Dylan claims that he is entitled to the €50,000 that his mother intended to leave to him but Jeff disagrees. Advise Jeff and Dylan as to the terms and validity of the will.

questions.text: In October 2008, Monica had to go into hospital for a serious heart operation. She decided that since there were so many risks involved that she should make a will in case anything went wrong. Monica was quite nervous at the thought of the operation so the nurse put her under light sedation. In the meantime, her solicitor Joey came into the hospital to help with drawing up the will. Since Monica was feeling a bit drowsy, she asked Joey to sign the bottom of the will for her. Joey did so with Monica's name and also signed his own name. He then asked the doctor and nurse who were tending to the patient in the bed next to Monica's to also sign. Neither the doctor or the nurse looked at the document they just signed it as they were engaged in deep conversation. Monica left all her worldly belongings to her husband Mike (worth approximately €500,000). She made no provision for her daughter, Cheryl, as she felt that she was old enough to look after herself now and besides, she had supported her for years while in University. In August 2010, Monica discovered that Mike was having an affair with his secretary and threw him out of the house. She phoned Joey, her solicitor, and asked him to destroy the will she made in 2008 and rewrite a new one, leaving her entire estate to the cats and dogs home. Following the phone call, Joey tore up the will and left it in a bag for shredding. He then drew up the new will, as requested, for Monica. However, that evening, Monica was knocked down and killed while walking the dog. She had not signed the new will. Mike and Cheryl both claim that they should be entitled to something from Monica's estate. Advise Mike and Cheryl.

Kenneth has suggested that perhaps his sister is entitled to it. Advise Breda.

In light of the above statement, outline and critically assess the system for recording property transactions in relation to property in Ireland.

questions.text: In 2000, Tom, a farmer, sold one of his fields to Jigsaw Developments Ltd. The field was adjacent to a main road which the local authorities had indicated would be upgraded to motorway status in the near future. Jigsaw Developments Ltd bought the property from Tom with the intention of building a large motorway service station on the site. Once the sale of the land was complete, Jigsaw Developments Ltd wrote a letter to Tom asking him if he could remove his sheep from the field and requesting him to place one of their advertising signs on the gate nearest the road. However, Tom, being a fairly laid back type of person, left these tasks on the long finger. In fact, due to various delays in the motorway development, Tom continued to graze his sheep on the land for a number of years. In 2004, Tom decided that a change was needed and he left farming. He sold his sheep and retired to the Canary Islands. Before he left, he made an agreement to lease the field to his neighbour, Joe, so that he could grow some mushrooms. Joe erected a number of structures in the field to serve as growing rooms. In 2007, the Chief Executive of Jigsaw Developments Ltd was driving by the field and noticed that there was some kind of funny looking structure in it. He parked the car and looked in over the gate at the structure in the field. He decided to notify his solicitor who wrote to Joe requesting that he leave this private property immediately. Joe replied and said he had intended on leaving anyway as he was getting tired of the mushroom farming and in any case, more traffic on a motorway would ruin his crops. At the beginning of this year, Jigsaw Developments Ltd has finally started on the motorway development but Joe has decided he wants to remain on the land to try and grow more organic vegetables. Advise Jigsaw Developments Ltd.

questions.text: In March 2013 Diane who was a fit and healthy 28-year-old, noticed that she had an unusual mark on her skin. Upon further investigation at the hospital, it was determined that Diane had skin melanoma (cancer), which needed to be removed as soon as possible and further treatment would be required. Before Diane went into hospital for treatment, her boyfriend Mark, who was a solicitor, suggested that it may be a good idea to draw up a will since she had three year old twins. Being a positive thinker and not really thinking too much – about it, Diane decided to draft a will for the sake of keeping Mark happy. She asked Mark to act as her solicitor by drawing up a preliminary will leaving all of her worldly items to her twin sons. Mark did so and dropped it by her house for her signature the week before the operation was due to take place. However, a few days later Diane entered a competition on the Weekend Show and won a new car with €20,000 in the glovebox. She decided that it would be important to include this in her will also. When Mark called to the house the day before her operation to collect the signed will, Diane signed the original will document and added in handwriting the car and the money. She did this in the presence of Mark and her twin boys. Her neighbour Sheila was also walking into the room when Diane was initialising the will. "Sheila you need to see this too!" Mark called out to her. "I can see you signing it Diane" she replied as she had her back turned pouring the tea. Later that evening, Sheila and Diane were alone sitting by the fire. The fire was nearly gone out so Sheila threw some old newspapers into the fire to try and keep it going. It was only after she threw in the newspaper that she noticed that the documents of the will were stuck in between the papers. She quickly removed the will from the fire, with the document being only partially burned. Advise as to the terms and validity of the will.

Rachel was very nervous in the days leading up to the procedure so the doctor gave her a light sedative. In the meantime, Rachel's good friend, Peter who was a solicitor, came by the hospital to see her and to talk to her about drawing up the will. Since Rachel was acting a bit drowsy, Peter offered to sign the bottom of the will for her. With Rachel's consent, Peter did so with Rachel's name, while at the same time, signing his own name. He then asked the doctor and nurse who were tending to the patient in the bed next to Rachel's to also sign the document. Neither the doctor or the nurse looked at the document - they just signed it as they were engaged in deep conversation with their patient. Rachel left everything she had to her husband John (worth approximately €500,000). She made no provision for: her daughter, Mandy, since she had supported her for years while in boarding school. In August 2012, Rachel discovered that John was having an affair with his secretary and threw him out of the house. She phoned Peter, her solicitor, and asked him to destroy the will she made in 2010 and to write a new one, leaving her entire estate to the cats and dogs home. Following the phone call, Peter tore up the will and left it in a bag for shredding. He then drew up the new will as requested by Rachel. However, that evening, Rachel was knocked down and killed while walking the dog. She had not signed the new will. John and Mandy both claim that they should be entitled to something from Rachel's estate. Advise John and Mandy.

Consider whether the Residential Tenancies Act 2004 has succeeded in its aim of providing a clear legislative framework in respect of tenancy obligations for landlords and tenants in Ireland.

questions.description: In 2000, Joe, a farmer, sold one of his fields to a large commercial development company - RJ Developments Ltd. The fiel…
questions.text: In 2000, Joe, a farmer, sold one of his fields to a large commercial development company - RJ Developments Ltd. The field was adjacent to a main road which the local authority had indicated would be upgraded to motorway status at some stage in the future. RJ Developments Ltd purchased the property from Joe with the intention of building a large motorway service station on the site. Once the sale of the land was complete, RJ Developments Ltd e-mailed Joe asking him if he could remove his cattle from the field and requesting him to place one of their advertising signs on the gate nearest the road. However, Joe never checked his e-mails since he did not 'do that technological stuff'. In fact, due to various delays in the motorway development, Joe continued to graze his cattle on the land for a number of years'. In 2006, Joe decided that a change was needed and he left farming. He sold his cattle and went on a round the world trip. Before he left, he made an agreement to lease the field to his best friend and neighbour, Frank, so that he could grow some mushrooms. Frank put a number of mushroom tunnels in the field to serve as growing rooms. In 2009, the Chief Executive of RJ Developments Ltd was driving by the field and noticed that there was some kind of funny looking structure in it. He parked the car and looked in over the gate at the structure in the field. He decided to notify his solicitor who wrote to Frank requesting that he leave this private property immediately. Frank replied and said he had intended on leaving anyway as he was getting tired of the mushroom farming and in any case, more traffic on a motorway would ruin his crops. At the beginning of this year, RJ Developments Ltd has finally started on the motorway development but Frank has decided he wants to remain on the land to continue to grow organic vegetables, which have been flourishing in the field. Advise RJ Developments Ltd.

This was never increased. Over the years, Mat became increasingly dependent on Lucy for things that went above and beyond the role of housekeeper. In fact, Lucy had become Mat's full-time carer rather than his housekeeper. Despite the fact that there was no conversation to this effect, Lucy claims that Mat had led her to believe that upon his death, Lucy would be entitled to the house or at the very least, she would have an unequivocal right to remain in it. Lucy stated that this was a mutual understanding between the parties. Mat has now died and has left all of his property to a local charity. Lucy seeks your advice on whether she has a claim to any interest in the house.

questions.text: In 1980, Jenny built a small house on a site, much of which was bogland, adjacent to Fred's land. The conservatory of the house had a large bay window that overlooked Fred's land which was a large field with some really beautiful flowers and a stream at the end of it. The only access route to the nearby road is through Fred's field, because the driveway which Jenny built on her own land is almost always flooded during the year. Jenny has therefore continually used the field since 1982. This year, Fred has sold the field to a developer whose plans for development include the erection of a 12 foot high boundary wall. The wall will cut off the field access to the road and it will considerably reduce the amount of light received through the bay window and destroy Jenny's beautiful view. Advise Jenny.

questions.text: Answer (a) and (b) Julie died a widow last month. By her will, she left all of her property to her only son, John. However, John died last year, survived by his wife Jacinta and his son, Mike. By his will, John left all of his property to Jacinta. Discuss. And One morning, following a news report of yet another natural disaster causing numerous deaths, Jenny decided that she had better draft her will before something happened to her. She had no paper so she wrote out her will on the back of a box of Weetabix. Instead of her signature which she didn't like the look of, she marked the end of the will with an "X". Later that morning, her two closest friends called over, Tom and Jerry. Jenny pointed out to them where she had signed the will and asked them to sign as witnesses.  Jerry,  who  was celebrating his  18th birthday  that week,  received  a mobile  phone  call  from  the bakery, after which he left in a hurry before he could sign the will. Jenny was anxious to get her will finalised and so called over to Jerry's house that evening to obtain his signature. Jerry signed the will as requested. A couple of days later, Fred, a neighbour, told Jenny that her will was not valid as it was written in pencil. Jenny replied "You might as well tear it up and throw it away then". Fred crumpled the will up in a ball and left it on the table. A couple of days later Jenny was killed in a tragic car accident. Discuss the validity of Jenny's will.

In January 2002, Paddy, a student, noticed the’ apartment was unoccupied for some time and decided to move in. He redecorated it and continued to live in the apartment until he finished his degree in May 2004. Claire, a friend of Paddy's, moved into the apartment the day after he left. In December 2013, Claire received a letter from James' solicitor requiring her to vacate the apartment as soon as possible. Claire ignored the letter and continued to occupy the apartment. At the end of January 2014, Claire received a second letter asking her to vacate the premises as soon as possible. Advise Claire as to what rights, if any, she has over the apartment.

questions.text: Dominic owns a large farm in Co. Longford. In 2006, he purchased the property next door to his farm which contained an old dilapidated cottage. Dominic used the old cottage to store farm tools and hay bales. During the summer months, he permitted passing holiday-makers to camp in the grounds of the cottage. Hans, one of the campers, decided to explore the cottage one summer afternoon. As he entered the house, he stumbled and got his foot caught inside a rotten floorboard. As he was trying to release his foot, he noticed something shiny at his foot. He discovered a gun (which later turned out to be an old antique) and a large sum of money. He brought the discovery to the attention of the Gardaí, who then proceeded to question Dominic. Dominic, believing that there may be some illegality attached to the gun, denied any knowledge of the find. In the absence of any further evidence, the Gardaí retained the gun for further investigation, but returned the money to Hans. Upon discovering this, Dominic now argues that the money belongs to him. Advise Hans.

questions.text: In 2000, Janet decided to start growing some organic vegetables in a field behind her house. After she had them all planted, she had a small shed erected to store her gardening tools in. A couple of months later, Janet planted a large hedge along the boundary to act as a windbreak. John, the registered owner of the field, lived in Spain. While holidaying in Ireland, John drove past the field only to see all the work that Janet had done on it. Janet happened to be out pulling up some weeds at the time. John told Janet that he had no present use for the field so she could continue her great work but he did intend to seek planning permission to build a house on it in the future. In the meantime, John told Janet that he would be happy to grant her a short lease. Janet asked him to write to her solicitor about this but despite the fact that negotiations followed, no lease was ever granted. Janet died in 2012, leaving all her property to her daughter, Sheila, who had resided with her. John died in 2008, leaving all his property to his son Dermot. Dermot is in poor health and lives in a nursing home. Sheila seeks your advice on whether or not she can be registered as owner of the field.

questions.text: One bright spring morning, Matthew went out for a ten mile jog on his land. Matthew paid little attention to this but soon after, he tripped and fell over something jutting out of the ground. Upon closer inspection, he realised that it was an antique gold pendant which had an engraving on the back "To my true love T'. Bob, the owner of the field in question, was out checking on his cattle and upon hearing the noise, ran over to Matthew to check if he was ok. He noticed the pendant in his hand covered in mud and claimed that it belonged to his great great grandfather - Trevor. Advise the parties as to who is entitled to the pendant.

questions.text: Anne and Barry got married in 2007. They moved into their new home in Kerry located on a 100-acre farm in the countryside. The family home and the farm were both in Barry's name. In 2010, Anne discovered that Barry was having an extra­marital affair and so she moved back in with her parents in Dublin. Barry threw himself into his farm work and decided that an expansion of the business would be a good idea. He applied to take out a mortgage with the Bank. When the bank asked him if he was married, Barry replied 'My wife is dead to me'. The bank took this literally and suggested that Barry would have to sign the statutory declaration to say that his wife was deceased in order for them to proceed with his application. Since 2010, Barry has lost all interest in the farm. This resulted in his defaulting on the mortgage instalments. Anne became aware through Barry's sister that the Bank intends to sell the farm including the family home. Anne is furious and claims she never consented to this mortgage on the property. Advise the Bank.

questions.text: In 1970, Peter built a house on a site adjacent to Ken's land. Since the driveway of this house was liable to serious flooding, Peter asked Ken in 1971 if he could pass through Ken's field as his normal way of access to the main roadway. Ken said that he had no problem with that. Peter has continued to use this alternative access route ever since, although there was one period of six months in 2001 when he tried an alternative way over a field belonging to another neighbour. However, that proved unsatisfactory and Peter reverted to crossing Ken's field. In January 1993, Peter built an extension to his house, consisting of a new kitchen with one large window overlooking Ken's field. The field contains some beautiful old oak trees, which was why Peter positioned the window where he did. This year, Ken has sold the field to a developer whose plans for development include the erection of a ten-foot high boundary wall. The wall will not only cut off Peter's access to the road via the field, but it will considerably reduce the amount of light received through the kitchen window destroying the view in the process. Advise Peter.

questions.text: When Jenny discovered that she had terminal cancer in March 2013, she decided to draft a will. She didn't have the energy to go to a solicitor and so asked her civil partner of two years, Sarah, to pick up a will template in the local post office. Jenny asked the neighbours, Maggie and Tom, over that evening for dinner with her and Sarah. After dinner, and with Sarah and the neighbours present, Jenny started drafting a number of versions of her will. Once she was happy with its contents - €100, 000 to her partner, Sarah, €50,000 to her niece, Lisa and the remainder of her estate to the cats and dogs home, Maggie and Tom duly signed their names to the will. Once the neighbours went home, Jenny started to tidy up. She accidentally ripped up the will thinking it was one of the old versions and threw it in the bin. The next morning when Sarah realised Jenny's mistake, she cello-taped the will back together again and put it in the drawer. In July 2013, Jenny sadly passed away. When Sarah brings the will to a local solicitor's office, she is met with questions concerning its content and validity. Advise Sarah.

questions.text: In January 2006, Jenny, Michael, Julie and Tom were students attending University. They won the lottery and decided to use their winnings to purchase a house together so that they could all live in it while they completed their studies. The purchase price was €100,000. Jenny contributed €40,000 and the others each paid €20,000 towards the purchase price. They agreed that the house would belong to them all equally and it was registered in all of their names. Later that year, Jenny failed to submit an assignment, the result of which was she had to leave the course. She sold her interest in the house to Dominic, a friend. Michael got married that summer and made a will leaving all of his property to his wife Janet. They went on honeymoon to a desert island where Michael was caught up and killed by a freak wave. In the meantime, relations broke down between Julie and Tom and Julie decided that she wanted to sell her share in the property. Tom was very upset by this and a big row followed. Julie sought legal advice and acting on it, she sent notice of severance by registered post to Tom. When the notice arrived, Tom was out and so Julie signed for it. Julie thinks it is best to sell the property altogether but Tom and Dominic want to stay in the house. Janet wants the share that her husband Michael left her in his will. Advise the parties.

questions.text: 1n 1999 Dan began to cultivate a field behind his house. He planted carrots and potatoes and later he built a shed to store his gardening tools and equipment. He also planted a hedge along the edge of the field to act as a windbreak. Ned who currently lives in Jamaica is the owner of the field in question. One day, while Ned was back in Ireland on holidays, he decided to check in on his field. He met Dan out in his garden and told him that while he had no present use for the field, he did intend to build on it in the future. Meanwhile, he told Dan that he was prepared to grant him a short lease for the time being. Dan contacted his solicitor about this and although some negotiations to conclude a lease followed, nothing ever came of it. Dan died in 2003, leaving all of his property to his son Jim, who lived with him. Ned died in 2007, leaving all of his property to his daughter Tracy. Tracy is in poor health and lives in a residential home. Now, Jim seeks your advice on whether or not he can retain the field as he would like to apply for an extension to the house. Advise Jim.

questions.description: In 2007, John was diagnosed with terminal pancreatic cancer and so he decided that he would make a will. He decided to l…
questions.text: In 2007, John was diagnosed with terminal pancreatic cancer and so he decided that he would make a will. He decided to leave his entire estate (worth €5,000,000) to his wife, Marian, and daughter, Fiona in equal shares. He did not leave anything to his estranged son, Ollie (a twin brother of Fiona) who he had not seen in the past 10 years. John's solicitor, Bob, advised him that he would draw up the will and bring it in to be signed in few days. However, John was dosed up on high levels of morphine at the time but he signed the will nonetheless. Bob was present when John signed the will and witnessed him making his signature. A family member of the patient in the bed next to him who was passing at the time also witnessed the will but while John was signing the family member momentarily had her back turned while she puffed up her father's pillow. However, she was there while John signed his name. Upon John's death, a family dispute erupted. Ollie returned home to find out about his inheritance. Upon learning that he received nothing from his father's estate, he decided he was going to contest it. Mari is extremely concerned that the family home which she has lived in for the past 30 years will now have to be sold so that Fiona can be given her share of the estate. She would also like to know whether Ollie has a chance of being successful. Marian decides to look for some legal advice. Advise Marian as to the validity of the will and on any other relevant issues which may affect the distribution of John's estate.

He dusted it off and saw that the back of it there was an engraving “To my dearest S, with all my love always and forever”. Paddy dropped the brooch into his pocket. Just then, Sean, who sold Bob the land ten years ago, appeared in the tunnel with Bob and claimed that he should have the brooch as it belonged to him. questions.description: Jackie died suddenly from a short illness in January 2012. At the time of her death, she was living on the family farm w…
questions.text: Jackie died suddenly from a short illness in January 2012. At the time of her death, she was living on the family farm with her husband Frederick (which she had inherited some 20 years previous). In her will, Jackie left all of her worldly belongings to her son, Ken. Jackie also has a daughter from a previous relationship to whom she left nothing under the will. Upon discovering her mother’s death, Julie claims she was left out of the will as she did not get along with her mother. However, she does acknowledge that her mother gave her €15,000 10 years ago to start up a new business venture which never took off. In fact Julie suffered from alcohol and drug addiction at the time and mishandled the money as a result. Julie has been in recovery for the past five years and is currently unemployed and living in local authority housing. Ken, on the other hand, is married with three children and is a wealthy dentist. Advise Ken and Julie.

questions.description: In 2009, Ben was informed after a routine doctor’s check –up that he was in serious bad health and needed to start takin…
questions.text: In 2009, Ben was informed after a routine doctor’s check –up that he was in serious bad health and needed to start taking more care of himself.  He decided that it was time to make a will so that those he cared about would be adequately provided for should anything terrible happen to him.  He instructed his good friend Norris, a local solicitor, as to the terms of the will. Norris drew up the will accordingly. A couple of weeks later, Ben suffered a stroke which left him paralysed on the left side of his body. Norris visited Ben in hospital with the will in his possession. However, Ben was unable to sign it himself since he was left - handed. Nonetheless, he asked Norris to sign the will on his behalf. There were two young people visiting the person in the bed next to Ben’s and Norris asked if they would mind signing the will also. Ben left his entire estate (worth approximately €6 million) to his wife, Deirdre, who was 10 years his junior. Ben had been married to Jane, who died in 1984. Ben and Jane had one child, Tracey, while married. No reference was made to Tracey in Ben’s will. However, this was mainly due to the fact that Ben had already purchased a duplex apartment for Tracey which was worth over €1 million. Furthermore, he had paid for her education and contributed a substantial sum to her now-thriving florist business. Ben recovered from his illness. However, he soon discovered that his wife Deirdre had been having an affair with the local butcher. This resulted in a serious row between the couple in March 2011 and Ben phoned Norris and asked him to “cut Deirdre out of the will immediately and give it all to Tracey”. Norris tore up the original will and drafted a new will as per Ben’s instructions over the phone. Two days later, Ben suffered a heart attack from the stress of the situation and died without signing the new will. Both Deirdre and Tracey now claim that Ben’s estate is rightfully theirs. Advise Deirdre and Tracey.

questions.description: According to the Property Registration Authority of Ireland “We have now reached a position where some 93% of total land…
questions.text: According to the Property Registration Authority of Ireland “We have now reached a position where some 93% of total land mass, and comprising 88% of the legal titles in Ireland, are now registered in the Land Registry. The System of recording deeds, in respect of the remaining titles, operated by the Registry of Deeds, is gradually reducing and will, ultimately, be of interest to historians and genealogists only”. In light of the above statement, outline and critically assess the system for recording property transactions in relation to property in Ireland.

questions.description: In 1998, Jimmy, a farmer, sold one of his fields to ZigZag Developments Ltd.  The Field was adjacent to a main road whic…
questions.text: In 1998, Jimmy, a farmer, sold one of his fields to ZigZag Developments Ltd.  The Field was adjacent to a main road which the local authorities had indicated would be upgraded to motorway status in the near future. ZigZag Developments Ltd bought the property from Jimmy with the intention of building a large motorway service station on the site. Once the sale of the land was complete, ZigZag Developments Ltd wrote a letter to Jimmy asking him if he could remove his sheep from the field and requesting him to place one of their advertising signs on the gate nearest the road. However, Jimmy being a fairly laid back type of person, left these tasks on the long finger. In fact, due to various delays in the motorway development, Jimmy continued to graze his sheep on the land for a number of years. In 2004, Jimmy decided that a change was needed and he left farming. He sold his sheep and retired to the Canary Islands. Before he left, he made an agreement to lease the field to his neighbour, Joe, so he could grow some mushrooms.  Joe erected a number of structures in the field to serve as growing rooms. In 2007, the Chief Executive of ZigZag Developments Ltd was driving by the field and noticed that there was some kind of funny looking structure in it.  He parked the car and looked in over the gate at the structure in the field. He decided to notify his solicitor who wrote to Joe requesting that he leave this private property immediately. Joe replied and said he had intended on leaving anyway as he was getting tired of the mushroom farming and in any case, more traffic on a motorway would ruin his crops. At the beginning of this year, ZigZag Developments Ltd has finally started on the motorway development but Joe has decided he wants to remain on the land to try and grow more organic vegetables. Advise ZigZag Developments Ltd.

questions.text: Geraldine is 55 years old and for the past 30 years she has been employed as a housekeeper for Emily at Emily’s house. When she first started working for Emily, Geraldine received £35 a week and this was never increased. Over the years, Emily became increasingly dependent on Geraldine for things that went above and beyond the role of housekeeper. In fact Geraldine had become Emily’s carer rather than her housekeeper. Despite the fact that there was no conversation to this effect, Geraldine claims that Emily had led her to believe that upon her death, Geraldine would be entitled to the house or at the very least a right to remain in it. Geraldine stated that this was a mutual understanding between the parties. Emily has now died and has left all of her property to the cats and dogs home. Geraldine seeks your advice on whether she has a claim to any interest in the house.

questions.description: In 1980, Mat built a bungalow on a site, much of which was bogland, adjacent to Sean’s land. The kitchen of the house ha…
questions.text: In 1980, Mat built a bungalow on a site, much of which was bogland, adjacent to Sean’s land. The kitchen of the house had one window that overlooked Sean’s land which was a large field with some really beautiful woods at the end of it.  The only realistic means of access to the nearby road is through Sean’s field, because the driveway which Mat built on his own land is almost flooded. Mat has therefore continually used the field since 1982. This year, Sean has sold the field to a developer whose plans for development include the erection of a ten foot high boundary wall. The wall will cut off the field access to the road and it will considerably reduce the amount of light received through the kitchen window and destroy Mat’s view of the woods. Advise Mat.

questions.description: In 2007, four close college friends, David, Paul, Mathew and Alex, bought a duplex apartment together. Title to the apar…
questions.text: In 2007, four close college friends, David, Paul, Mathew and Alex, bought a duplex apartment together. Title to the apartment was not registered in the Land Registry. They each paid an equal share of the purchase price. Shortly afterwards, David lost his job and decided to emigrate and sold his interest to Paul. In 2008, Paul unexpectedly became a father and decided, without legal advice, that it was a good time to make a will whereby he would leave his entire share in the property to his son. In 2009, Mathew moved out and rented a room in a house a few doors away because he was finding it very difficult to study for his final year exams in the apartment due to elevated noise levels. This caused an argument between Alex and Paul which resulted in Paul moving all of Alex’s possessions, including his plasma screen television and his x-box, from the living room and locking the living room door. On hearing this, Alex’s girlfriend suggested that he live with her in her house and so Alex then moved out. Mathew and Alex believe that Paul should pay them rent. Alternatively, Mathew suggests that the apartment should be sold. Alex is uncertain as to whether the apartment should be sold because its market value has fallen a great deal over the past couple of years due to the recession. Paul says that he is not willing to pay them rent and that they are welcome to stay in the apartment. He objects to selling the apartment because he will not be able to find another home in the vicinity that is suitable for him given its close proximity to his place of work. He is also concerned as to whether his would be adequately provided if he were to does unexpectedly. Advise the parties.

questions.description: The succession Act 1965 “...is based on the idea that a testator owes a duty to leave part of his estate to his widow (t…
questions.text: The succession Act 1965 “...is based on the idea that a testator owes a duty to leave part of his estate to his widow (the legal right) and to make proper provision for his children in accordance with his means. It is not based on a duty to provide maintenance for his widow nor is it limited in its application to children who were dependent on him”. Per Kenny J, FM v. TAM (1972) 106 ILTR 82, 86. Critically discuss this statement with reference to case law.

questions.description: Janet is the daughter of Mildred who recently passed away after a life long battle with cancer. When Mildred was 55 year…
questions.text: Janet is the daughter of Mildred who recently passed away after a life long battle with cancer. When Mildred was 55 years old, Janet gave up her job in a local solicitors firm to move in and care for her mother. She continued to do this until Mildred died when she was 87 years of age. Mildred was disabled as a result of her illness and there was an informal agreement that Mildred’s illness benefit would be paid to Janet to meet the needs of them both. Janet also received carer’s allowance for looking after her mother. All the bills were paid out of Mildred’s savings. Mildred said on many occasion to Janet “I really don’t know how I would get on without you taking care of me. When I am gone, you will have a secure home”. Janet assumed this meant Mildred would leave her the property. However, Mildred died intestate and has left five children. Under the rules of intestacy, the house will be divided amongst them all equally. Janet seeks your advice on whether she has any rights in relation to the property which are binding on her siblings? Would your answer be any different if the other children had signed a contract 10 years ago clearly stating that when Mildred died, that they would allow Janet to continue living in the house?

Consider whether the Residential Tenancies Act 2004 has succeeded in its aim of providing a clear legislative framework for tenancy obligations for landlords and tenants in Ireland.

questions.description: One Friday evening, while out exploring with his dog on his father’s land, 12 year old Simon discovered a hidden entranc…
questions.text: One Friday evening, while out exploring with his dog on his father’s land, 12 year old Simon discovered a hidden entrance to a cave which was accessible from both his father’s land and that of his neighbour, Mr. Burns. Simon and his dog walked over a cardboard sign on the ground which read “Keep Out- No Trespassing”. However, Simon and the dog carried on regardless and ventured further into the cave. After a few minutes, Simon discovered a gold brooch and some other pieces of old fashioned jewellery which were half-buried in the ground. He wiped off the mud and saw that on the back of it there was an engraving “All my love to my dearest T, always and forever”. Simon dropped the brooch and the other artefacts into his pocket of his jacket. Just then, Tim, who had sold Simon’s father the land ten years previously, appeared in the cave with Simon’s father and claimed that he should have the find as it belonged to him. Advise the parties as to who is entitled to the brooch.
