questions.description: Lesley did last month from severe heart attack, leaving a net estate worth 500,000. In 2005, Lesley had made a will. The…
questions.text: Lesley did last month from severe heart attack, leaving a net estate worth 500,000. In 2005, Lesley had made a will. The only bequest in the will was one of “50,000 to my best friend, Jimmy Jones”. However, in the will, the figure 40,000 had been crossed out and the figure “100,000” written above it in black biro (the rest of the will had been typewritten). Lesley’s solicitor now admits that there was a mistake in the drafting of the will. The week after Lesley had first given instructions as to the terms of the will, the solicitor’s secretary had received a phone call from Lesley saying that he felt he should leave more to Jimmy Jones to 100,000. Unfortunately, the secretary, who was leaving to go on holidays that day, totally forgot to pass the message on to the solicitor. The solicitor is not sure how the figure of 100,000 came to appear on the will but believes that it is in the testator’s handwriting. One of the witnesses to the will, Delilah, was cohabiting with Jimmy when the will was executed and the couple were married to months later. Lesley was a bachelor and had no children. Lesley’s parents both died twenty years ago. However, he was survived by one of his sisters, Julie, with whom he had lost touch. His other twin sisters, Jackie and Ellie, died ten years ago. At the time of Lesley’s death, Jackie was survived by her children, Jack and Jill, and Ellie was survived by her two grandchildren, Tom and Jerry. Advise as to proper distribution of Lesley’s estate.

questions.description: In March 2010, Anita, who was an elderly lady, discovered that she had a lump in her leg.  Upon further investigation at…
questions.text: In March 2010, Anita, who was an elderly lady, discovered that she had a lump in her leg.  Upon further investigation at the hospital, it was decided that the lump was cancerous, should be removed as soon as possible and further treatment would be required.  Before Anita went into hospital for treatment, she decided that she had better draw up a will making provision for her husband, Brian.  She didn’t feel there was any need to include her son, Dylan as she believed that when Brian died, Dylan would ultimately inherit everything. Anita asked her solicitor, Liz, if she could draw up the will according to her instructions and drop it by her house for signature the week before the operation was due to take place.  However, a few days later Anita won €50,000 on a scratch card which she collected.  She decided that it would be important to include this in her will also.  When Liz called to the house with the will, Anita signed the will in the presence of Liz and Dylan.  Brian was walking into the room next door when Anita was initialling the will.  “I think you are supposed to watch me doing this love!” Anita called out to him.  “I can see you signing it darling” he replied as he had his back turned.  After Liz left the house, Anita suddenly remembered the money that she had won on the scratch card and added it to the bottom of the will, indicating that it was to go to Dylan.  She thought that she should get her signature witnessed so asked both Dylan and his friend (who happened to be there at the time) to sign the will.  Later that evening, Brian came in and sat by the fire.  It was nearly gone out so he threw some old newspaper into the fire to try and keep it going.  The will which was stuck in the newspaper was thrown into the fire with the newspaper and destroyed. That night, Anita died in her sleep.  Dylan claims that he is entitled to the €50,000 that his mother intended to leave to him but Brian disagrees. Advise Brian and Dylan as to the terms and validity of the will.

questions.description: Tom passed away in April 2010.  His estate consisted of a substantial farm in North Cork.  In his will, Tom left his est…
questions.text: Tom passed away in April 2010.  His estate consisted of a substantial farm in North Cork.  In his will, Tom left his estate “...to my three children: Tod, Fran and Jim”.  Soon after the death of their father, Tod has a serious fight with Fran and Jim over what was to be done with their father’s estate.  Tod, refusing to leave the farm idle, took possession of the farm and has worked it ever since.  As a result of the fight, Tod refused to talk to Fran or Jim or allow them onto the property.  He was of the opinion that he was the only one who ever cared about their father in recent years and so he should have been left the farm in its entirety in any case. In the last year, both Fran and Jim have lost their jobs and want to sell their respective shares in the property to Tod.  However, they cannot reach agreement with Tod over the purchase price as Tod feels it should be sold at a lower price.  A local property developer has expressed an interest in the land and Fran and Jim think that it is a good idea to sell the farm.  However, Tod opposes the sale. Advise the parties, supporting your answer with relevant authority.

questions.description: After a night out, John decided to take a short cut home through a meadow which was beside his house.  While trespassing…
questions.text: After a night out, John decided to take a short cut home through a meadow which was beside his house.  While trespassing on this property, he tripped over something which was half buried in the soil.  Upon further investigation, he realised that he had discovered what appeared to be a very valuable silver chalice.  Janet, who had taken out a ninety-nine year lease on the meadow the previous year, claimed that she should be entitled to the find.  As word got around the local village, Janet’s landlord, Simon, heard about the find and claimed that since he owned the field, he should be entitled to the chalice.  However, John now claims that since he was the one to discover the chalice, he should be entitled to keep it. Discuss.

questions.text: Fred has issued a new lease to George of cellars which lie below and adjacent to property retained by him.  The lease agreement specifically states that the cellars will be used for a new tapas bar.  However, upon inspection of these cellars, the local Health Authority has stated that the ventilation system which is in place is wholly inadequate and a new ventilation system will have to be installed.  George has acted accordingly and hired a company to install the new system which involves fixing new piping to the property that has been retained by the landlord.  Fred immediately questions George’s right to do this and now seeks your advice. Discuss with reference to relevant case law.

questions.description: In May 2010, Blanche went on holidays in the West of Ireland.  One afternoon, she took her dog for a walk in what she be…
questions.text: In May 2010, Blanche went on holidays in the West of Ireland.  One afternoon, she took her dog for a walk in what she believed was a private garden owned by the B&B in which she is staying.  While strolling around, she stumbled on something which appeared to be sticking out from the ground.  She discovered a large bronze brooch which was covered in soil from the ground.  Blanche was always of the belief that “finders keepers, losers’ weepers”.  However since she is an honest person, she decides to tell the owner of the B&B, Kenneth, who informs her that the garden doesn’t actually belong to him but his sister Julia took out a 99-year lease on the land a couple of years ago. Kenneth suggests that perhaps his sister is probably entitled to it. Advise Blanche.

questions.description: In October 2008, Monica had to go into hospital for a serious heat operation.  She decided that since there were so many…
questions.text: In October 2008, Monica had to go into hospital for a serious heat operation.  She decided that since there were so many risks involved that she should make a will in case anything went wrong.  Monica was quite nervous at the thought of the operation so the nurse put her under light sedation.  In the meantime, her solicitor Jack came into the hospital to help with drawing up the will.  Since Monica was feeling a bit drowsy, she asked Jack to sign the bottom of the will for her.  Jack did so with Monica’s name and also signed his own name.  He then asked the doctor and nurse who were tending to the patient in the bed next to Monica’s to also sign.  Neither the doctor or the nurse looked at the document – they just signed it as they were engaged in deep conversation. Monica left all her worldly belongings to her husband Mike (worth approximately €500,000).  She made no provision for her daughter, Cheryl, as she felt that she was old enough to look after herself and besides, she had supported her for years while in University. In August 2010, Monica discovered that Mike was having an affair with his secretary and threw him out of the house.  She phoned Jack, her solicitor, and asked him to destroy the will she made in 2008 and rewrite a new one, leaving her entire estate to the cats and dogs home.  Following the phone call, Jack tore up the will and left it in a bag for shredding.  He then drew up the new will as requested for Monica.  However, that evening, Monica was knocked down and killed while walking the dog.  She had not signed the new will. Mike and Cheryl both claim that they should be entitled to something from Monica’s estate. Advise Mike and Cheryl.

questions.description: Stephen died of cancer in January 2010. In 2007, he was diagnosed with lung cancer and so he decided that he should make…
questions.text: Stephen died of cancer in January 2010. In 2007, he was diagnosed with lung cancer and so he decided that he should make a will in case the treatment prescribed for him did not work.  He decided to leave his entire estate (worth €1,500,000) to his wife, Celia, and daughter, Lauren in equal shares.  He did not leave anything to his son, Dylan (a twin brother of Lauren) who had spent the last few years travelling the world, working his way from one place to the next.  However, not long before he died, Stephen decided that he should probably leave Dylan something in his will as he was his only son. Stephen contacted his solicitor, Ben, who advised him that it would be unnecessary to draw up an entirely new will and that a codicil was all that would be required under the circumstances.  A week later, Ben dropped into the hospital with the codicil which stated that Dylan was to get €200,000.  However, Stephen was dosed up on high levels of morphine at the time but he signed the codicil nonetheless.  Ben was present when Stephen signed the will and witnessed him making a signature.  A hospital nurse who was passing at the time also witnessed the will but while Stephen was signing she momentarily had her back turned while she tended a patient.  However, she was there while Ben signed his name. Upon Stephen’s death, a family dispute erupted.  Dylan was really upset that he had only been left €200,000 while his twin sister had been left so much more.  He had never wanted much off his father and started working from a very young age unlike Lauren.  Celia is extremely concerned that the family home which she has lived in for the past 30 years will now have to be sold so that Dylan and Lauren can be given their shares of the estate.  Celia decides to look for some legal advice. Advise Celia as to the validity of the will and codicil and on any other relevant issues which may affect the distribution of Stephen’s estate.

questions.description: In 1980, Jenny inherited a small cottage from her uncle.  She visited the property on a couple of occasions but decided …
questions.text: In 1980, Jenny inherited a small cottage from her uncle.  She visited the property on a couple of occasions but decided that she did not wish to live there as it was too quiet and isolated and her job and friends were located in the city.  Nonetheless, she decided to keep the house as somewhere to go for a break some weekends, a holiday home in the summer and ultimately a retirement home.  She asked the neighbour next door – Charles – to ‘keep an eye’ on the cottage for her and gave him a spare key in the event that an emergency arose. However, work became very busy for Jenny that year, the result of which was that she never had the chance to stay in the cottage.  A couple of years later, Charles began using the shed at the rear of the cottage to store his bicycle and his garden tools.  In 1985 he decided to carry out some repairs on the shed as well as the grounds which had become quite dilapidated and in a state of disrepair.  This work, which included a new gate and padlock (for which he had the only key) cost him €500.  Since Jenny had not been in contact with Charles, he never told her about this.  A year later, Charles started to use the house to accommodate friends who came to visit.  He also started to rent it out during the summer months and kept the money for himself.  In 2005, Charles sold his land to Jack and handed over the keys to Jenny’s house.  He informed Jack that Jenny had not been around for years.  Jack continued to use Jenny’s property as if it was his own.  In early 2010, Jenny returned to her cottage and demanded her keys back. Advise Jack.

With regard to the general scheme of the Act – for example, its provisions on improved security of tenure; its new tenancy termination procedures; and the establishment of the Private Residential Tenancies Board as a dispute resolution mechanism – critically assess whether the Residential Tenancies Act 2004 has succeeded in its objective of providing a clear statutory framework for the tenancy obligation of landlords and tenants in Ireland.

questions.description: Betty was the fee simple owner in freehold of The Pines, a large house with extensive surrounding grounds.  Betty used T…
questions.text: Betty was the fee simple owner in freehold of The Pines, a large house with extensive surrounding grounds.  Betty used The Pines as a bed and breakfast business.  In 1999, a local businessman, Tom, asked Betty if she might be interested in selling part of the grounds attached to The Pines.  Tom intended to build a restaurant on the land.  Betty was agreeable but in the conveyance with Tom she ensured that Tom covenanted on behalf of himself and his heirs and his assigns that he would refrain from using the premises for anything other than a restaurant.  The conveyance, which was registered, also contained a covenant requiring Tom to maintain the driveway shared by the properties.  Finally, it was expressly stated in the conveyance that the covenants were made “for the benefit of Betty and all future owners of The Pines”. In December 2006, Betty sold The Pines to Patricia.  In April 2007, Tom sold the restaurant to his nephew, Peter.  Peter, anxious to develop the business, began to provide live music for patrons, which, on summer nights, soon turned into a feature on the restaurant’s business and regularly led to impromptu parties being held on the restaurant’s decking/patio area.  Patricia’s customers began to complain about the noise and recently one of them posted a long commentary on a popular travel website advising that it was now probably best to avoid Patricia’s B&B during the holiday season.  In addition, the customer noted that generally standards had fallen at Patricia’s B&B with the driveway now looking no better than a “forgotten country lane”. Last week, after a particularly raucous “theme night” at the restaurant, Patricia decided to confront Peter.  She reminded him: “when you bought that place from your uncle you signed up to the same obligations as he had.”  Peter coolly replied: “the only thing I signed was the cheque to buy this restaurant and if the noise is bothering your customers then you should invest in double glazing.” Advise Patricia.

questions.description: Daniel owns the fee simple in a house in Co. Cork.  The property, which is Daniel’s only asset and is unregistered, was …
questions.text: Daniel owns the fee simple in a house in Co. Cork.  The property, which is Daniel’s only asset and is unregistered, was valued at €300,000 when Daniel inherited it from his uncle in 2006. In the winter of 2008 after a long night in the local pub, Daniel fell into a conversation with the local auctioneer, James.  James told Daniel that he was involved in a new retail development in the city, and that it was a “sure thing” that money would be made from the project.  James recommended that Daniel should invest at least €400,000 in the scheme, and that Daniel should approach a number of local banks to see if they might lend him the money. The next day Daniel made an appointment with his local bank, Fitzpatrick’s Lenders, and asked for a loan of €300,000.  The bank manager, Sean, agreed and asked Daniel to deposit the title deeds to his house with Fitzpatrick’s as security for the loan.  Daniel deposited the title deeds with Fitzpatrick’s but a month later he called Sean and told him that he needed the title deeds for his solicitor as he was drawing up his will.  Sean gave the deeds to Daniel.  Daniel drove to Limerick and approached Easicredit Bank for a mortgage of €100,000.  The manager, Breffni, glanced at the title deeds and asked Daniel if there were any other mortgages secured on the property.  “No, thank God” replied Daniel.  Breffni agreed to approve the mortgage, and Daniel signed a mortgage deed to secure the loan with Easicredit. Daniel transferred the €400,000 to James in June of this year but has not been able to contact James since August when James said that he was going away for a “short break”.  Daniel has been struggling to meet the mortgage repayments to Fitzpatrick’s and Easicredit.  In fact, he has not made any repayments since August.  Sean and Breffni are worried about their exposure to the loans involved. Advise the banks as to their respective rights in law.

questions.description: In H v O’C and others [2007] IEHC 68, Herbert J stated that in determining issues arising in applications pursuant to se…
questions.text: In H v O’C and others [2007] IEHC 68, Herbert J stated that in determining issues arising in applications pursuant to section 117 of the Succession Act 1965 the High Court must: act justly, fairly and impartially, to avoid the greatest extent possible perpetuating or deepening any feelings of suspicion or hostility which the litigation had engendered among family members; and remember that the test to be applied was not which of the various courses open to the testator the court itself would have adopted if confronted with the same situation, but rather, whether the decision of the testator to opt for the course did of itself, and without more, constitute a breach of moral duty to the claimant. What do you think of Herbert J’s approach to section 117 applications?  Support your answer with reference to relevant case law, principle and commentary.

questions.description: Henry had been seriously ill for some weeks before he finally died in June of this year.  In his will, which he made in …
questions.text: Henry had been seriously ill for some weeks before he finally died in June of this year.  In his will, which he made in 2006, he left his entire estate (worth €1.5 million) to his wife Karen and his son Hector in equal shares.  When making his will in 2006, Henry had been estranged from his daughter Patti, a single mother, who lived in a commune in Mayo.  In the Christmas before he died, Henry had a chance encounter with Patti, and her son Eoin.  The meeting deeply affected Henry and he sought to amend his will.  Henry’s solicitor, Bertie, advised him that he did not need to make a completely new will and that a codicil would suffice in the circumstances.  Henry duly instructed Bertie to draft a codicil leaving €100,000 to Patti. Barite managed to have the codicil ready by the end of May of this year.  By that stage, Henry was hospitalised and under heavy medication.  Barite advised the hospital to get Henry to sign the codicil.  The nurse on duty expressed some doubts as to whether Henry would know what was about to happen.  Barite, who is a solicitor of 30 years’ standing, politely acknowledged her advice and then asked the nurse to witness the will.  On seeing Bertie, Henry appeared to rally himself and signed his name to the codicil in Bertie’s presence.  While this was going on the nurse was called away to tend to another patient and only returned after Bertie had signed his name. Following Henry’s death, a family dispute arose.  Patti was disappointed that she was left with less than 10% of Henry’s estate, and that nothing had been passed to Eoin as Henry’s only grandchild.  Hector was livid that Patti had received so much, and has told a friend that he suspects “emotional blackmail” on Patti’s part.  Karen is concerned that the family home (worth €700,000) will have to be sold in order to give Hector and Patti their share of the estate.  Karen seeks legal advice. Advise Karen on the validity of the will and codicil and on any other issues that may affect the distribution of Henry’s estate.

questions.description: In 1993, the Munster Development Corporation (MDC), a statutory body charged with the promotion of industrial developmen…
questions.text: In 1993, the Munster Development Corporation (MDC), a statutory body charged with the promotion of industrial developments in the province, purchased a field near Limerick City with the intention of selling it in the future with the benefit of planning permission.  The purchase of the field made the local newspapers and media because it was felt at the time the MDC had paid well over the going market rate for the field.  In June 1995, cattle belonging to the neighbouring farmer, Eoin, broke into the field.  Eoin noticed that the fencing on the field was in bad repair and that the land appeared to be lying idle.  Eoin decided there and then to leave his cattle to graze on the field.  A few months later, Eoin repaired the fence and was soon maintaining the fencing on a regular basis, as well as spreading fertilizer on the land.  In 2000, Eoin carried out expensive drainage work on the land.  In 2002, he retired from farming and transferred whatever right, title or interest he had in the field to his son, Ray, who has been farming the land since.  In June 2007, MDC put up a site notice indicating that a planning permission application for the erection of a factory had been lodged with the local authority.  Ray recently noticed a “For Sale” sign on the property and wrote to the estate agents involved with an offer to purchase the property.  He has recently heard that he may have “squatter’s rights” to the property, and seeks legal advice on the matter. Advise Ray.

questions.description: In McGinness v Sherry & Anor [2008] IEHC 134, the High Court considered an application seeking directions as to the prec…
questions.text: In McGinness v Sherry & Anor [2008] IEHC 134, the High Court considered an application seeking directions as to the precise meaning and import of a bequest contained in a will.  On reviewing the “several rules of construction” used by the Irish courts in the process of interpreting the terms of a will, Mac Menamin J cited a specific rule which suggested: “Where it is clear on the fact [or face] of a will that the testator has not accurately or completely expressed his meaning by the words he has used, and it is also clear what are the words he has omitted, those words may be supplied in order to affect the intention [of the testator], as collected from the context.” Given the above: Outline and discuss generally the various rules of construction that are used by the Irish courts in attempting to give effect to the ambiguous terms of a will or imprecise parts thereof; and In your answer, make particular reference to the balance that must be struck between an interpretation that simply removes a possible ambiguity in a will and that which might involve the re-writing of a will.

questions.description: Joe McEvoy made his will in June 2004.  He died in July 2007, survived by his two sons, Daniel and Martin, and his daugh…
questions.text: Joe McEvoy made his will in June 2004.  He died in July 2007, survived by his two sons, Daniel and Martin, and his daughter Marie.  Probate of his will was granted in July 2008.  By that will, John made the following bequests: “My 50 acre farm at Blackacre and my house at Black Hill to my son Daniel absolutely.  My land at Whiteacre to my son Daniel absolutely but my daughter Marie may take a site from this property for the purpose of building a house there on.  It is my wish that my daughter would not sell this site.  My bank account at Eire Bank, I leave to my son Martin absolutely. All the residue and remainder of my estate to be equally divided between my two sons and daughter.” In 2004, the house at Black Hill and the adjacent land at Blackacre were valued at €200,000.  It is now valued at €270,000.  The land at Whiteacre which is adjacent to the River Shannon is a small holding of no more than two acres.  It was valued at €65,000 in 2004 and is now valued at €75,000.  The bank account amounts to €50,000. There was no residue to divide, as it went on funeral expenses. Daniel, the youngest child, is now in his mid-thirties, and lives alone at Black Hill.  He has suffered from regular bouts of depression since childhood but a recent change in medication appears to be working well. The farmland at Blackacre is not of good quality, and Daniel is grateful to supplement his income with his weekly disability benefit.  Martin has been living in San Francisco for the past ten years.  Thanks in no small part to an investment of €15,000 made by his later father in 1998 Martin’s hairdressing business is thriving.  He kept in regular contact with his father and remains on good terms with Daniel but has not spoken to Marie since the unexpected death of their mother in 1999. Marie is unhappy with the outcome of the will and writes to you to seek advice on challenging its terms.  In the letter she informs you of what she calls “four points of interest”.  First, she says, although her father funded her education, which led her to her career as a social worker, she has not seen eye-to-eye with him since 1999.  She thinks that this may have had to do with the fact that she refused to return to the family home when her mother died.  Second, she notes that since her father died her diabetes has dis-improved appreciably due to a combination of work-related stress and a “slight” drink problem.  Third, she claims that the site at Whiteacre “isn’t worth a curse” due to the fact that it is prone to flooding.  Finally, she includes in her letter a clipping from a recent edition of the local newspaper, which reports that the route of the planned motorway through the locality is likely to be adjusted in order to avoid the ruins of an old Abbey, and that a number of local farmers appear set to benefit from the “windfall” of the various compulsory purchases orders that will in all likelihood be needed to develop the road.  From the plans published in the paper, it now appears that the motorway will edge onto Blackwater. Her letter ends by stating that, although she would not like to “unduly upset” Daniel, he is now “sitting on a goldmine” and that this is “clearly unfair”. Advise Marie.

questions.text: De Londras (Principles of Irish Property Law, 2007 at p.327) is of the view that the complexity associated with the law of mortgages is rooted in equity’s insistence on fairness for the mortgagor, which has resulted in a situation in which a mortgage on paper and a mortgage in practice are almost entirely different things: on paper it seems that the mortgagee owns the mortgaged property, whereas in practice the mortgagor continues (in all most all contexts) to be treated as the owner. What is your opinion of this perspective on the Irish law of mortgages?  Support your answer with relevant case law and principle.

questions.description: Betty died in April 2007.  Betty’s estate consisted of a large plot of farmland in North County Dublin.  By her will, Be…
questions.text: Betty died in April 2007.  Betty’s estate consisted of a large plot of farmland in North County Dublin.  By her will, Betty left her entire estate “to my four children: Daniel; Anthony, James and Priscilla.”  On Betty’s death, Daniel took possession of the farm and has managed it ever since.  Controversially, and despite several attempts, Daniel has not permitted his brothers and sister to enter the farm since the death of their mother.  Daniel is of the view that the farm should not have been left in co-ownership but should have been left to him in its entirety on the ground that he is the eldest son and eldest child. Recently, Anthony, James and Priscilla grew weary of the rows surrounding the co-ownership of the family farm and resolved to sell their respective shares in the property to Daniel.  Through an independent party, they met with Daniel on several occasions but were unable to reach an agreement, principally because Daniel refused to pay the current market value of the property. Last month, Priscilla was killed in a road accident.  Two weeks ago, Anthony was declared bankrupt.  James is distraught and now wants the issue of the family farm sorted as soon as possible.  A local property developer has made soundings about the possibility of acquiring the property.  James would like to sell the farm to the developer but Daniel is, unsurprisingly, strongly against the proposed sale. Advise James of the difficulties that might arise in procuring a sale of the entire farm, and how they might be overcome, if at all.

questions.description: The Law Reform Commission’s recent report on the law of landlord and tenant in Ireland (LRC 85-2007), sought inter alia …
questions.text: The Law Reform Commission’s recent report on the law of landlord and tenant in Ireland (LRC 85-2007), sought inter alia to clarify the extent of the general obligations of the landlord and tenant, in particular where the parties fail to make specific provision in the lease for such obligations, and including the rules terminating a tenancy agreement. In light of that approach by the LRC, critically examine the current status of Irish landlord and tenant law?

questions.description: In Re NSM Deceased [1973] 1 ILTR 107, the testator left the residue of his estate to his son.  Because of the incidence …
questions.text: In Re NSM Deceased [1973] 1 ILTR 107, the testator left the residue of his estate to his son.  Because of the incidence of estate duty and the costs of litigation, nothing remained for the son.  Pursuant to an application under section 117 of the Succession Act 1965, the court held that the testator had failed in his moral duty to the son, Kenny J stating: “The court must attribute to the testator on the day before his death, knowledge of the amount of estate duty which will be payable on his estate and a remarkable capacity to anticipate the cost of litigation which will follow his death.  I realise that this is unreal, that the amount of estate duty payable is usually mercifully hidden from most testators and that it is impossible to anticipate what litigation will follow on death.  I am convinced, however, that section 117 must be interpreted in this way.” In light of more recent case law, do you remain convinced that the testator’s moral duty under section 117 should be interpreted in the manner outlined by Kenny J in Re NSM Deceased?

questions.description: Katie, a successful entrepreneur, made her will in 2006.  She had always been of the opinion that solicitors overcharge …
questions.text: Katie, a successful entrepreneur, made her will in 2006.  She had always been of the opinion that solicitors overcharge and consequently, after doing an internet search, she drafted her will on her computer at work.  She printed the will at her office and stamped the back of it with her personalised office seal, which she used regularly for her correspondence.  Her friend, Trevor, and Trevor’s wife, Katherine, signed the will.  Katie’s business partner, Tim, was named as executor of the will. The will left €35,000 to Trevor and €15,000 to Tim.  The residue of the estate went in equal shares to Katie’s daughter, Lisa, and Katie’s eldest son, Peter.  Last year Lisa, a single parent, was killed in a skiing accident.  Lisa’s son, Brian, is currently in Peter’s care.  Following the accident, Katie wrote, initialled and dated the following sentence in pencil after Trevor’s and Katherine’s signature on the will: “I would very much like that Lisa’s original share in this will goes to her beloved son Brian”.  In December of last year, Tim retired from the business.  In the aftermath of his retirement party, an emotional Katie wrote the following in the margin of the will: “I leave Tim €35,000 – not €15,000 – because he has been so supportive of me for so many years.”  The following weekend Katie died. Katie is survived by her husband, Jimmy, her sons, Peter and Mark, and her grandson, Brian.  Jimmy is the owner of a construction company, which is in severe financial difficulties.  Mark has a PhD in biochemistry and is employed by a leading pharmaceutical company in London.  Both Jimmy and Mark were upset to learn that they have, in Jimmy’s words, “effectively been disinherited”.  They approach Peter and ask him to divide his share of the estate amongst the three of them.  Peter has refused saying that it was not his fault that he was his mother’s favourite and that in any event he now has Brian to take care of. Tim, as executor of the will, is uncertain as to what to do.  The value of Katie’s estate is estimated at approximately €3.4 million and includes a family home worth €850,000. Advise Tim.
